subject: Advanced Materials
executive_summary:
Advanced materials -- encompassing metamaterials, carbon fiber composites, thermal protection systems, and rare earth elements -- represent a foundational technology layer underpinning virtually every defense system from hypersonic weapons to stealth aircraft to autonomous vehicles. The U.S. Defense Composites Market was valued at $10.3 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach $15.51 billion by 2032. The global metamaterials market is projected to grow from $1.55 billion to $17.44 billion by 2035, driven by defense stealth, sensing, and electromagnetic applications. China has made significant breakthroughs in thermal protection materials for hypersonic flight, while the United States maintains advantages in aerospace-grade carbon fiber production and metamaterial design, though supply chain vulnerabilities remain acute.

The most critical supply chain risk lies in rare earth elements and critical minerals. China controls 48% of proven global rare earth reserves, 70% of mining output, and holds a virtual monopoly over heavy rare earth processing. In December 2025, China imposed its strictest-ever rare earth and permanent magnet export controls through Announcement No. 61, categorically prohibiting exports of dual-use items to U.S. military end users. While these restrictions were temporarily paused until November 2026, China's underlying export-control architecture remains intact. The U.S. has responded with a $400 million equity stake in MP Materials and commitments to purchase 100% of its rare earth magnets, growing domestic refined rare earth production by 400% year-on-year in 2024. However, U.S. import reliance only dropped from 95% to 80%, leaving significant vulnerability.

China's breakthroughs in hypersonic thermal protection materials represent a particularly concerning development. Chinese scientists have created carbide ceramic materials capable of withstanding 3,600 degrees Celsius in oxidizing environments, exceeding current thermal limits on hypersonic flight, and developed innovative cooling devices for managing hypersonic heat. The 2025 unveiling of the YJ-17 hypersonic glide vehicle, capable of sustained Mach 10 speeds, demonstrates the operational significance of these materials advances. Meanwhile, U.S. efforts to establish domestic advanced materials capacity face headwinds from tariff impacts on carbon fiber imports (increased to 25% on raw carbon fiber tow) and persistent rare earth processing gaps.

key_findings.headline: China imposes strictest-ever rare earth export controls targeting U.S. military end users
key_findings.detail: China's Ministry of Commerce Announcement No. 61 (December 2025) implements the most restrictive rare earth and permanent magnet export controls to date, covering 12 of 17 rare earth elements. Companies with any affiliation to foreign militaries are largely denied export licenses. The restrictions also extend to tungsten and antimony. While temporarily paused until November 2026, the Article 1 prohibition on dual-use exports to U.S. military end users remains in force.

key_findings.headline: China develops thermal protection materials exceeding hypersonic flight limits
key_findings.detail: Chinese scientists created a carbide ceramic material capable of withstanding 3,600 degrees Celsius in oxidizing environments, surpassing current thermal limits for hypersonic flight. They also developed an innovative cooling device for managing hypersonic heat. These materials advances enabled the 2025 unveiling of the YJ-17 hypersonic glide vehicle capable of sustained Mach 10 speeds with advanced mid-course maneuvering.

key_findings.headline: U.S. rare earth production grows 400% but import reliance remains at 80%
key_findings.detail: U.S. production of refined rare earth minerals grew by over 400% year-on-year in 2024, reducing import reliance from 95% to 80%. The government invested $400 million in an equity stake in MP Materials, provided a 10-year floor price guarantee, and committed to purchasing 100% of its rare earth magnets, plus $150 million for heavy rare earth separation. However, the 80% import dependence remains a critical vulnerability.

key_findings.headline: Metamaterials market poised for explosive growth driven by defense stealth applications
key_findings.detail: The global metamaterials market is projected to grow from $1.55 billion to $17.44 billion by 2035 at a 27.36% CAGR, driven primarily by defense applications. Multi-frequency programmable metamaterials for adaptive stealth, radar-absorbing structures, and electromagnetic wave manipulation are key growth areas. Chinese researchers are actively publishing on radar cross-section reduction and thermal signature management using metamaterial designs.

key_findings.headline: U.S. defense composites market valued at $10.3 billion with 5.6% CAGR growth
key_findings.detail: The U.S. Defense Composites Market was valued at $10.3 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach $15.51 billion by 2032. Carbon fiber reinforced polymers (CFRP) are widely used in military drones, helicopters, fighter jets, and UAVs. The increasing use of UAVs for combat, surveillance, and reconnaissance is driving demand for lightweight, high-strength composite materials.

key_findings.headline: Carbon fiber tariffs increased to 25% as U.S. seeks to regionalize supply chains
key_findings.detail: Effective March 2025, U.S. tariffs on carbon fiber from targeted countries increased from 7.5% to 25% for raw carbon fiber tow and from 4.2% to 17.5% for prepreg materials. While aimed at reducing Chinese dependency, these tariffs also increase costs for U.S. defense manufacturers. Chinese carbon fiber capacity has surpassed 100,000 metric tonnes annually, though aerospace-grade quality gaps persist.

key_findings.headline: Kratos wins $68 million contract for next-generation hypersonic materials testing center
key_findings.detail: In October 2025, Kratos Defense secured a $68.3 million contract to establish a next-generation hypersonic materials-testing center. Lockheed Martin maintains leadership in high-speed flight thermal protection and guidance systems. The U.S. Army's Dark Eagle hypersonic system is scheduled to reach initial operational capability by end of FY2025, though progress has been slower than China's demonstrated capabilities.

recent_developments.headline: China implements strictest rare earth export controls in history
recent_developments.summary: China's Ministry of Commerce Announcement No. 61 implemented the most restrictive rare earth and permanent magnet export controls to date, covering 12 of 17 rare earth elements and extending to tungsten and antimony. Companies with military affiliations are categorically denied export licenses. While temporarily paused until November 2026, the underlying control architecture remains intact and the dual-use prohibition on U.S. military end users continues.
recent_developments.impact: Directly threatens U.S. defense supply chains for fighter jet engines, missile guidance systems, satellite components, and other systems requiring rare earth permanent magnets, with no near-term domestic alternative at scale.

recent_developments.headline: U.S. secures rare earth supply chain with $550 million MP Materials investment
recent_developments.summary: The U.S. government announced a $400 million equity stake in MP Materials along with a 10-year floor price guarantee and commitment to purchase 100% of rare earth magnets. An additional $150 million was provided for heavy rare earth element separation. U.S. refined rare earth production grew 400% year-on-year in 2024, reducing import reliance from 95% to 80%.
recent_developments.impact: Significant progress in onshoring rare earth processing, but 80% import reliance remains a critical vulnerability, especially for heavy rare earth elements where China maintains near-monopoly control.

recent_developments.headline: Kratos wins $68 million contract for hypersonic materials testing facility
recent_developments.summary: Kratos Defense secured a $68.3 million contract to establish a next-generation hypersonic materials-testing center, reflecting growing U.S. investment in thermal protection systems for hypersonic weapons. This comes as China has demonstrated carbide ceramic materials capable of 3,600-degree Celsius tolerance and unveiled the YJ-17 HGV capable of sustained Mach 10 speeds.
recent_developments.impact: Strengthens U.S. testing infrastructure for hypersonic materials but highlights the gap between Chinese demonstrated capabilities and U.S. developmental programs.

recent_developments.headline: Metamaterials market analysis projects $17.44 billion by 2035
recent_developments.summary: Market analysis report projects the global metamaterials market growing from $1.55 billion to $17.44 billion by 2035 at a 27.36% CAGR. Defense and aerospace applications including adaptive stealth, radar absorption, and electromagnetic wave manipulation are primary growth drivers. Research on programmable metamaterials for multi-frequency stealth is accelerating in both the U.S. and China.
recent_developments.impact: Metamaterials are becoming essential for next-generation stealth and sensing capabilities, with significant implications for the future balance of air superiority between U.S. and Chinese forces.

risk_assessment.current_risk: Advanced materials present a mixed risk picture with critical vulnerabilities in rare earth supply chains and hypersonic thermal protection. China's control of 70% of rare earth mining output and near-monopoly on heavy rare earth processing creates an acute dependency that the $550 million MP Materials investment has only partially addressed (U.S. import reliance dropped from 95% to 80%). China's December 2025 export controls directly target U.S. military end users, and the temporary pause until November 2026 provides only a narrow window for alternative sourcing. In hypersonic materials, China has demonstrated thermal protection capabilities (3,600C carbide ceramics) that exceed current U.S. specifications, enabling the YJ-17 HGV at Mach 10. The U.S. maintains advantages in aerospace-grade carbon fiber composites and metamaterial design, but Chinese capacity (100,000+ metric tonnes of carbon fiber annually) is closing the gap. Defense-relevant tariff increases (25% on raw carbon fiber) may help onshore production but increase near-term costs.
key_drivers: China's rare earth export controls (Announcement No. 61) directly targeting U.S. military end users
80% U.S. import reliance on rare earth elements despite 400% production growth
Chinese breakthroughs in hypersonic thermal protection materials (3,600C carbide ceramics)
Near-monopoly Chinese control of heavy rare earth element processing
Expanding Chinese carbon fiber production capacity (100,000+ metric tonnes annually)
Growing metamaterials research in China for adaptive stealth applications
Extension of Chinese export controls to tungsten and antimony critical minerals
mitigation_actions: Accelerate domestic rare earth processing capacity beyond MP Materials, including heavy rare earth separation
Diversify rare earth sourcing through partnerships with Australia, Canada, and African mining projects
Increase investment in hypersonic thermal protection R&D to match Chinese demonstrated capabilities
Establish strategic reserves of critical defense materials including rare earth magnets, tungsten, and antimony
Support domestic carbon fiber manufacturing expansion for aerospace-grade production
Fund metamaterials research at national laboratories and defense universities
Develop rare earth recycling programs for end-of-life defense systems to recover critical materials
